posts.name: Мајкл Ристев
posts.cooked: i am always curious about this demographic of consumer. surely even the most wealthy of individuals would know better after just a moderate amount of tasting experience. perhaps overzealous noobs are the primary drivers of unicorn fetishism in the market?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
i am always curious about this demographic of consumer. surely even the most wealthy of individuals would know better after just a moderate amount of tasting experience. perhaps overzealous noobs are the primary drivers of unicorn fetishism in the market?

Given that in e.g. 2014 (one of the best renditions) Cédric only produced 150 bottles of this cuvée, it would be difficult to achieve even a “moderate amount of tasting experience” with this particular wine! Given the microscopic production, it’s probably the least consistent wine in the range, vintage to vintage; but when it’s on it’s simply remarkable.

posts.name: E. de Koning
posts.cooked:
William,

Do you know how many bottles are produced in 2017?

Etienne de Koning

Given that in e.g. 2014 (one of the best renditions) Cédric only produced 150 bottles of this cuvée, it would be difficult to achieve even a “moderate amount of tasting experience” with this particular wine! Given the microscopic production, it’s probably the least consistent wine in the range, vintage to vintage; but when it’s on it’s simply remarkable.

How many times have you been able to taste this cuvée William? And of those how many have been “on”?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
i am always curious about this demographic of consumer. surely even the most wealthy of individuals would know better after just a moderate amount of tasting experience. perhaps overzealous noobs are the primary drivers of unicorn fetishism in the market?

Given that in e.g. 2014 (one of the best renditions) Cédric only produced 150 bottles of this cuvée, it would be difficult to achieve even a “moderate amount of tasting experience” with this particular wine! Given the microscopic production, it’s probably the least consistent wine in the range, vintage to vintage; but when it’s on it’s simply remarkable.

How many times have you been able to taste this cuvée William? And of those how many have been “on”?

I guess around twenty times, a handful of which have been among the best rosé Champagnes I ever tasted!

Cédric’s ex-domaine pricing does not reflect what happens on the secondary market afterwards, and I suspect he is actually tempted just to drink this cuvée himself given how it is blowing up.

posts.name: Мајкл Ристев
posts.cooked:
i am always curious about this demographic of consumer. surely even the most wealthy of individuals would know better after just a moderate amount of tasting experience. perhaps overzealous noobs are the primary drivers of unicorn fetishism in the market?

Given that in e.g. 2014 (one of the best renditions) Cédric only produced 150 bottles of this cuvée, it would be difficult to achieve even a “moderate amount of tasting experience” with this particular wine! Given the microscopic production, it’s probably the least consistent wine in the range, vintage to vintage; but when it’s on it’s simply remarkable.

i suppose my point more so is that if one were to spend the $7-800 referenced by these auctions it would be possible taste the entire lineup of the bouchard wines [minus the rosé] even at less than stellar stateside pricing; this would most likely be far more instructive than beasting out over the rarest bird.

posts.name: Kelly Walker
posts.cooked: I have a bunch from both 2010 and 2014. Crazy.

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked: Thanks for the optimal aging info for the FacSimile, Charlie. Had one (current release) quite recently and was intrigued rather than wowed. The Creux d’Enfer is really remarkable when “on” and I’m lucky to have tried a few. I can see people paying those prices, given the scarcity, though I wouldn’t myself. If you love Rose Champagne, why not? There’s plenty of Ramonet Grand Cru that sells for a lot more and ends up down a drain…

posts.name: Brad Baker
posts.cooked: I’m with William on this wine. I always enjoy it and sometimes find it to be haunting (in a good way) and breathtaking. From a suggested retail price stand point in France, it is the highest priced wine in Cedric’s portfolio, but really not that much more than the other higher end Roses de Jeanne wines (Ursules, Presle, Boloree, Haute Lemble). I’m actually shocked that it has taken this long for demand to send the aftermarket price up. The wine is made in tiny quantities (normally only a few hundred bottles) and is such an elegant, yet powerful wine. Changes with age more than Cedric’s others wines too IMO.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
William,

Do you know how many bottles are produced in 2017?

Etienne de Koning

I will ask when I visit the day after tomorrow!

And William,
How was the visit?
Did you taste the line-up from 2017?
I am very curious for the 2017 vintage.

Thanks,
Etienne

250 bottles were produced in 2017! It’s the finest, best balanced rendition to date and I think it should age especially well.

The 2017 portfolio is superb: a bit fleshier and more outgoing than 2016, but not quite as rich and muscular as 2015—a great balance.

There is also some exciting news chez Cédric that I’ll be reporting on soon…

PS - We also did a vertical of Ursules from magnum, which tended to confirm my contention that, rather than “not evolving”, these Champagnes simply evolve like still wines: that’s to say, gaining depth, aromatic range and sapid nuance, but without picking up the roasted/toasted/caramelized aromas that come from Maillard-like reactions and the breakdown of sugar. 2006 in magnum right now is incredibly demonstrative and very hard indeed to resist (so hard to resist that I have opened one tonight which I’m sipping as I write this).
